sentences.base_language: David enrolled in a cooking class at the culinary school downtown, something he had wanted to do for years.
sentences.target_language: David se inscribió en una clase de cocina en la escuela culinaria del centro, algo que llevaba años queriendo hacer.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Llevar + time + gerundio** = to have been doing something for a period: **llevaba años queriendo** hacer (had been wanting to do for years). This construction expresses a continuous past action up to another past moment. Compare: **hacía años que quería** hacerlo — both are correct, but **llevar + gerundio** is more idiomatic in spoken Spanish.
**Inscribirse en** = to enroll in, to sign up for (reflexive): se **inscribió en** una clase (enrolled in a class). The reflexive **se** marks a voluntary, self-directed action. Compare: **matricularse** (to enroll formally in a course or school), **apuntarse** (to sign up — informal, Spain). All three take **en** before the course or activity.

sentences.base_language: When he arrived, the instructor — an experienced chef named Rosa — welcomed the group warmly.
sentences.target_language: Cuando llegó, la instructora — una chef experimentada llamada Rosa — recibió al grupo con mucho afecto.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Llamado/a** = named, called (past participle as adjective): una chef **llamada** Rosa (a chef named Rosa). This participial adjective agrees in gender and number with the noun: un chef **llamado** Rosa, una instructora **llamada** Rosa. Compare: **de nombre** Rosa (by the name of Rosa — more formal), **que se llamaba** Rosa (who was called Rosa — relative clause with imperfect).
**Recibir a alguien con** = to welcome/receive someone with: recibió al grupo **con** mucho afecto (welcomed the group warmly). **Con** + abstract noun replaces many adverbs: con **afecto** (warmly/affectionately), con **entusiasmo** (enthusiastically), con **amabilidad** (kindly). This construction is often more natural than the corresponding adverb: **afectuosamente**.

sentences.base_language: She announced that they would make a traditional three-course meal completely from scratch.
sentences.target_language: Anunció que prepararían una comida tradicional de tres tiempos completamente desde cero.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Conditional in reported speech**: **anunció que prepararían** (announced that they would prepare). When the reporting verb is in the past (**anunció**), the future shifts to the conditional in the subordinate clause: **prepararían** (they would prepare). This is the 'future of the past' or 'conditional of reported speech': dijo que **vendría** (said he would come), prometió que **lo haría** (promised she would do it).
**De tres tiempos** = three-course (meal): **tiempo** in culinary Spanish = a course of a meal: una comida de **tres tiempos** (a three-course meal — primer tiempo, segundo tiempo, postre). Compare: **plato** (dish/plate — individual dish within a course). **Desde cero** = from scratch — a calque of 'from zero': hacer algo **desde cero** (to do something from scratch, from nothing). Also used in tech and business: empezar **desde cero** (start from scratch).

sentences.base_language: David had never cooked anything more complex than scrambled eggs, so he felt both excited and nervous.
sentences.target_language: David nunca había cocinado nada más complicado que unos huevos revueltos, así que se sentía a la vez emocionado y nervioso.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Nunca + pluperfect** = had never: **nunca había cocinado** (had never cooked). The pluperfect (**había** + past participle) sets a background condition prior to the main narrative events. **Nunca** with perfect tenses precedes the auxiliary: **nunca había** hecho, **nunca he** visto. Compare: **jamás** (ever/never — emphatic): **jamás** había probado algo tan rico (had never tasted anything so delicious).
**A la vez** = at the same time, simultaneously — a fixed adverbial phrase: **a la vez** emocionado y nervioso (both excited and nervous at once). Compare: **al mismo tiempo** (at the same time — interchangeable), **a un tiempo** (literary register). **Sentirse + adjective** = to feel (internal state): se **sentía** nervioso (felt nervous — imperfect for a sustained background feeling). Compare: se **sintió** nervioso al oírlo (felt nervous upon hearing it — preterite for a sudden onset).

sentences.base_language: First, Rosa demonstrated how to hold a knife correctly to avoid cutting their fingers.
sentences.target_language: Primero, Rosa les demostró cómo sujetar un cuchillo correctamente para no cortarse los dedos.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Para + infinitivo** = in order to (purpose): **para no cortarse** los dedos (in order not to cut their fingers). When the subject of both clauses is the same, **para** + infinitive expresses purpose. When subjects differ, **para que** + subjunctive is required: les explicó el método **para que** no se cortaran (so that they wouldn't cut themselves). Note: **para no** (in order not to) vs **sin** (without doing): **sin cortarse** (without cutting themselves).
**Cortarse los dedos** — reflexive + body part: in Spanish, body parts are preceded by the definite article, and the possessor is indicated by the reflexive or indirect object pronoun: **se cortó el dedo** (cut his/her finger — not *su dedo*). This pattern is consistent: **me duele la cabeza** (my head hurts), **te lavaste las manos** (you washed your hands). The article replaces the possessive adjective when the owner is already clear from the pronoun.

sentences.base_language: She explained that most kitchen accidents happen because people do not respect the blade.
sentences.target_language: Explicó que la mayoría de los accidentes en la cocina ocurren porque la gente no respeta la hoja.
sentences.grammarNote:
**La mayoría de + definite article + noun** = the majority of, most: **la mayoría de los** accidentes (most accidents). The verb agrees with the noun after **de**, not with **mayoría**: **la mayoría de los** estudiantes **llegan** tarde (most students arrive late — plural). Compare: **la mayor parte de** (the greater part of — same construction and agreement rule): **la mayor parte del** tiempo (most of the time).
**Porque + indicativo** = because (stating a real cause): **porque** la gente no respeta (because people don't respect). Compare: **para que + subjuntivo** (so that — purpose): lo dijo **para que** tuvieran cuidado (she said it so they would be careful). **Porque** introduces a factual cause; **para que** introduces an intended result. **La hoja** = the blade (of a knife) — also means leaf (of a plant) or sheet (of paper): context disambiguates.

sentences.base_language: David practiced chopping onions and his eyes began to water almost immediately.
sentences.target_language: David practicó cortando cebollas y los ojos le empezaron a llorar casi de inmediato.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Practicar + gerundio** = to practice doing: **practicó cortando** (practiced chopping). **Practicar** takes a gerund when the practice involves doing a skill: **practicó hablando** en voz alta (practiced speaking aloud). Compare: **practicar** + noun (to practice a sport or discipline): **practicar** natación (to practice swimming as a sport). The gerund construction emphasizes the activity as ongoing practice.
**Llorar los ojos** = for the eyes to water (literally: to cry): **los ojos le empezaron a llorar** (his eyes started to water — lit. his eyes began to cry to him). The indirect object **le** marks whose eyes they are. **Empezar a + infinitivo** = to start to: **empezaron a** llorar (started to water). **Casi de inmediato** = almost immediately — **de inmediato** is a fixed adverbial phrase equivalent to **inmediatamente**: reaccionó **de inmediato** (reacted immediately).

sentences.base_language: The other students laughed kindly, and Rosa told them it was a sign the onions were very fresh.
sentences.target_language: Los demás estudiantes se rieron con cariño, y Rosa les dijo que era una señal de que las cebollas estaban muy frescas.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Los demás** = the others, the rest (of them): **los demás** estudiantes (the other students — i.e., everyone else in the group). **Demás** is invariable as a determiner and always follows the article: **los demás**, **las demás**. Compare: **otro/a** (another, a different one — singular, refers to one alternative): **otro** estudiante (another student — a different one). **Los demás** = the whole remaining group; **otros** = some others.
**Era una señal de que + indicativo** = it was a sign that (reported factual content): **era una señal de que** las cebollas estaban frescas. The **de que** (rather than just **que**) is required after nouns like **señal**, **prueba**, **hecho**, **idea**: la **idea de que** fuera fácil (the idea that it would be easy). This **de que** construction is called a 'complemento del nombre' and triggers the debate known as **dequeísmo** when incorrectly used with verbs.

sentences.base_language: For the main course, they prepared a slow-cooked stew with seasonal vegetables and tender meat.
sentences.target_language: Para el plato principal, prepararon un guiso a fuego lento con verduras de temporada y carne tierna.
sentences.grammarNote:
**A fuego lento** = over low heat, slow-cooked — a fixed culinary phrase: cocinar **a fuego lento** (to cook over low heat / to slow-cook). Compare: **a fuego alto** (over high heat), **a fuego medio** (over medium heat). The preposition **a** introduces a manner of cooking: **al vapor** (steamed), **al horno** (baked/roasted), **a la plancha** (griddled), **al baño maría** (bain-marie). These **a/al** + culinary mode phrases are essential kitchen vocabulary.
**De temporada** = in season, seasonal: verduras **de temporada** (seasonal vegetables — those currently in season). Compare: **de estación** (same meaning, more formal), **fuera de temporada** (out of season). **Tierna** (f.) = tender (adjective agreeing with **carne**, f.): **carne tierna** (tender meat). Note: **tierno/a** also means young/soft affectionately: **un niño tierno** (a sweet young child), **pan tierno** (soft/fresh bread).

sentences.base_language: Rosa walked around the room, tasting each person's dish and offering quiet suggestions.
sentences.target_language: Rosa fue recorriendo la sala, probando el plato de cada uno y haciendo sugerencias en voz baja.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Ir + gerundio** = a progressive action with a sense of gradual movement or sequence: **fue recorriendo** la sala (went around the room, making her way). **Ir + gerundio** adds a nuance of gradual, deliberate progression that **estar + gerundio** lacks: **fue explicando** cada paso (explained each step one by one). Compare: **estaba recorriendo** la sala (was walking around — simple ongoing action, no nuance of progression).
**En voz baja** = in a low voice, quietly: haciendo sugerencias **en voz baja** (making suggestions quietly). The opposite: **en voz alta** (aloud, in a loud voice). These are fixed expressions: **leer en voz alta** (read aloud), **hablar en voz baja** (speak softly). Compare: **en silencio** (in silence) and **a susurros** (in whispers). **Cada uno/a** = each one, each person: el plato de **cada uno** (each person's dish).

sentences.base_language: David accidentally added too much salt and his heart sank, but Rosa told him it was an easy mistake to fix.
sentences.target_language: David echó demasiada sal sin querer y se le cayó el alma a los pies, pero Rosa le dijo que era un error fácil de corregir.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Echar + ingredient** = to add (an ingredient, by pouring or throwing in): **echó** sal (added salt). **Echar** is the everyday culinary verb for adding ingredients: **echa** un poco de aceite (add a bit of oil), **échale** pimienta (put some pepper in it). Compare: **añadir** (to add — more formal/written), **agregar** (common in Latin America). **Sin querer** = accidentally, without meaning to — a fixed phrase: lo rompió **sin querer** (broke it accidentally). Opposite: **a propósito** / **adrede** (on purpose).
**Caérsele el alma a los pies** = to have one's heart sink (lit. for the soul to fall to one's feet) — an idiomatic expression of dismay: **se le cayó el alma a los pies** (his heart sank). Note the gustar-type indirect object **le** (to him) and reflexive **se**. **Fácil de + infinitivo** = easy to do: un error **fácil de** corregir (an easy mistake to fix). This construction uses **de** before the infinitive when an adjective qualifies how easy/hard/possible something is: **difícil de** entender (hard to understand), **imposible de** ignorar (impossible to ignore).

sentences.base_language: She showed him how to balance the flavors by adding a splash of lemon juice and a pinch of sugar.
sentences.target_language: Le enseñó cómo equilibrar los sabores añadiendo un chorrito de zumo de limón y una pizca de azúcar.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Enseñar a alguien cómo + infinitivo** = to show someone how to do: **le enseñó cómo** equilibrar (showed him how to balance). The indirect object **le** (to him) is obligatory with **enseñar** — you always teach *to* someone. Compare: **enseñar a + infinitivo** (to teach to do): **le enseñó a** cocinar (taught him to cook). **Cómo** introduces the manner/method; **a** introduces the learned skill.
**Un chorrito** = a splash, a small dash (diminutive of **chorro** — stream/jet): **un chorrito de** limón (a splash of lemon). Culinary diminutives convey small, imprecise quantities: **un chorrito** (a splash), **una pizca** (a pinch — the amount between two fingers), **una punta** (a tip/touch — very small amount), **un puñado** (a handful). These vague quantity words are indispensable in kitchen Spanish and resist exact translation.

sentences.base_language: The aroma from the pot filled the kitchen and made everyone's mouth water.
sentences.target_language: El aroma que salía de la olla llenó la cocina y a todos se les hizo la boca agua.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Hacérsele la boca agua** = for one's mouth to water — idiomatic: **se les hizo la boca agua** (their mouths watered — lit. the mouth made itself water to them). **A todos** is the indirect object (to all of them), fronted for emphasis. The full pattern: **a mí se me hace**, **a ti se te hace**, **a él/ella se le hace**, **a ellos se les hace la boca agua**. Compare: **se me antoja** (I'm craving), another indirect-object construction for desire.
**Que salía de** — relative clause with imperfect for continuous background action: el aroma **que salía de** la olla (the aroma that was coming from the pot — ongoing). The imperfect **salía** (was coming out) describes the ongoing emanation as background. Compare with preterite: el aroma **que salió** de la olla (the aroma that came out — at a specific moment). In descriptive clauses modifying nouns, the imperfect is typically used for ongoing states or actions.

sentences.base_language: By the time the food was ready, David's arms were tired from all the stirring.
sentences.target_language: Para cuando la comida estuvo lista, a David le dolían los brazos de tanto remover.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Para cuando + pretérito** = by the time (something happened): **para cuando** la comida estuvo lista (by the time the food was ready). **Para cuando** introduces a deadline reference point: **para cuando** llegues (by the time you arrive — future context, subjunctive). With a past reference: **para cuando** terminó (by the time it was done). Compare: **cuando** alone = when (at the moment that): **cuando** estuvo lista, la probaron (when it was ready, they tasted it).
**De tanto remover** = from all the stirring — causal **de tanto + infinitivo**: **de tanto** remover (from so much stirring). **Remover** in cooking = to stir (a pot): **remueve** bien (stir well). Do not confuse with English 'remove' — **remover** in Spanish means to stir/mix, not to take away. To remove/take away: **quitar**, **sacar**, **retirar**. A classic false cognate in kitchen contexts.

sentences.base_language: For dessert, they made a simple fruit tart that required careful attention to detail.
sentences.target_language: Para el postre, prepararon una tarta de frutas sencilla que requería mucha atención a los detalles.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Requería** — imperfect for a characteristic, ongoing requirement: **que requería** mucha atención (that required a lot of attention — as an inherent property). Compare: **requirió** (required — if describing the specific effort expended on that occasion, preterite). A relative clause describing a property of the noun typically uses the imperfect: una tarta **que requería** vs una tarta **que requirió** horas de trabajo (a tart that took hours of work — specific event).
**Atención a los detalles** = attention to detail: **atención a los detalles** is the standard phrase (plural in Spanish, unlike the English singular 'detail'). **Prestar atención** = to pay attention: **presta atención a los detalles** (pay attention to the details). Compare: **fijarse en** (to notice, to pay attention to): **fíjate en** los detalles (notice the details). **Sencillo/a** = simple, straightforward — often preferred over **simple** (which can sound dismissive): una receta **sencilla** (a straightforward recipe).

sentences.base_language: David felt a surge of pride when his tart came out of the oven perfectly golden.
sentences.target_language: David sintió un gran orgullo cuando su tarta salió del horno perfectamente dorada.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Sintió** — preterite of **sentir** for a sudden, discrete emotional experience: **sintió** un gran orgullo (felt a surge of pride — a specific moment). Compare: **sentía** (imperfect — for a sustained, background feeling): **sentía** mucho cariño por Rosa (felt a lot of affection for Rosa — ongoing). The preterite captures the sudden onset of emotion; the imperfect captures a lasting emotional state. **Sentir** (to feel — an emotion or sensation) vs **sentirse** (to feel — one's own state): **sintió** frío (felt cold — sensation) vs **se sentía** cansado (felt tired — his own state).
**Salir del horno** = to come out of the oven: **salió del horno** (came out of the oven). **Dorado/a** = golden (color and texture): **perfectamente dorado** (perfectly golden — the ideal bake). In cooking: **dorar** (to brown/to make golden) is the verb: **dora** la cebolla (brown the onion). **Dorado** as a culinary term always implies a desirable, appetizing color. Compare: **quemado** (burnt — undesirable), **crudo** (raw), **bien hecho** (well done).

sentences.base_language: The class sat down together to eat the meal they had prepared, feeling a warm sense of accomplishment.
sentences.target_language: La clase se sentó junta a comer la comida que habían preparado, con una cálida sensación de logro.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Junta** — adjective agreeing with **la clase** (f., treated as a collective singular): **se sentó junta** (sat down together). **Junto/a** as an adjective = together, unified: **estaban juntos** (they were together), **trabajaron juntos** (worked together). Compare: **juntos** as an adverb (invariable in this use): sometimes interchangeable, but when modifying the subject as an adjective, it agrees. **La clase** takes a singular verb here (collective noun treated as a unit).
**Que habían preparado** — pluperfect in a relative clause: the meal **that they had prepared** (completed before the act of eating). The pluperfect (**haber** imperfect + past participle) marks an action completed before another past action: **habían preparado** (had prepared — earlier) → **se sentaron a comer** (sat down to eat — later). **Con una sensación de logro** = with a sense of accomplishment: **logro** (m.) = achievement, accomplishment — from **lograr** (to achieve, to manage to do).

sentences.base_language: David discovered that cooking was more satisfying than he had expected.
sentences.target_language: David descubrió que cocinar era más satisfactorio de lo que había esperado.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Más … de lo que** = more … than (with a clause): **más satisfactorio de lo que** había esperado (more satisfying than he had expected). When the second term of a comparison is a clause (with a conjugated verb), Spanish uses **de lo que** (not **que**): **más difícil de lo que** creía (harder than I thought), **mejor de lo que** esperaba (better than expected). If the comparison is with a noun or simple adjective, use **que**: **más fácil que** eso (easier than that).
**Cocinar** as a noun (infinitive used as subject): **cocinar** era satisfactorio (cooking was satisfying). In Spanish, the infinitive functions as a verbal noun (gerund equivalent in English). Unlike English, which uses '-ing' forms as noun subjects (cooking, swimming), Spanish uses the infinitive: **nadar** es sano (swimming is healthy), **viajar** amplía la mente (traveling broadens the mind). The gerund (**cocinando**) cannot serve as the subject of a clause.

sentences.base_language: Rosa invited them all to return for the next class, which would focus on regional cuisine.
sentences.target_language: Rosa los invitó a todos a volver para la siguiente clase, que se centraría en la cocina regional.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Invitar a alguien a + infinitivo** = to invite someone to do: **los invitó a** volver (invited them to come back). The indirect/direct object (**los**) and the infinitive are both required: **los invitó a quedarse** (invited them to stay). Compare: **invitar a alguien a + place or event** (to invite to a place): **la invitó a cenar** (invited her to dinner — **cenar** as event, not 'to dine'). **A todos** reinforces the object — a redundant but emphatic addition: **los invitó a todos** (invited them all — every single one).
**Se centraría** — conditional in a relative clause after a past reporting context: **que se centraría en** (which would focus on — future-in-the-past). The conditional replaces the future when the context is anchored in the past: **dijo que vendría** (said he would come), **anunció una clase que trataría sobre** (announced a class that would deal with). **La cocina regional** = regional cuisine — **cocina** means both the physical kitchen and the cuisine/culinary tradition: **la cocina española** (Spanish cuisine), **la cocina de autor** (signature/haute cuisine).

sentences.base_language: David walked home with a full stomach and a new passion, already planning what he would cook the following weekend.
sentences.target_language: David volvió a casa con el estómago lleno y una nueva pasión, pensando ya en lo que cocinaría el fin de semana siguiente.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Pensando en lo que + conditional** = thinking about what he would: **pensando en lo que cocinaría** (thinking about what he would cook). **Lo que** = what (that which) — a free relative pronoun that introduces a clause: **lo que** quieras (whatever you want), **lo que** me dijiste (what you told me). Here it introduces the object of his thinking: he was planning **what he would cook**. The conditional **cocinaría** expresses future from a past perspective (planning ahead).
**El fin de semana siguiente** = the following weekend: **siguiente** (following, next — after a reference point) vs **próximo** (next — from now): **el fin de semana siguiente** (the weekend after that one in the story) vs **el próximo fin de semana** (next weekend — from the speaker's present). This distinction — **siguiente** for sequence within a narrative, **próximo** for proximity to now — is a subtle but important one for advanced learners. **Volver a casa** = to go back home, to head home — **volver** (to return) is more natural than **regresar** in most of Spain.
